_comment: Canonical seed data for Unlocking London. Single source of truth consumed by tools/directus/seed.mjs. Edit here, then re-run `npm run seed` in tools/directus.

review.verdict: Nose-to-tail cooking that defined modern British dining.
review.pull_quote: Bone marrow and a glass of claret - this is London on a plate.
review.body: Fergus Henderson's Smithfield institution still sets the standard for honest, seasonal British food. The whitewashed dining room is pure calm; the roast bone marrow with parsley salad is non-negotiable. Come hungry, bring your parents, order the Eccles cake and Lancashire cheese to finish.

places.title: Lyle's
places.address: Tea Building, 56 Shoreditch High Street, London E1 6JJ

review.verdict: Pared-back, ingredient-led cooking in a bright Shoreditch room.
review.pull_quote: A daily-changing menu that trusts the produce completely.
review.body: James Lowe's Michelin-starred canteen keeps things deliberately spare - white walls, a short set menu at dinner, and some of the best sourcing in the city. The natural wine list is a joy. Lunch is a la carte and a smart solo option; dinner is the full set-menu experience.

review.verdict: Bombay-cafe brunch worth every minute of the queue.
review.pull_quote: The bacon naan roll is a rite of passage.
review.body: The Shoreditch original leans into its Irani-cafe theme with a leafy courtyard and a first-floor bar. Brunch is the move: bacon naan rolls, akuri on toast, house chai. No bookings for small groups at peak, so arrive early or settle in with a chai while you wait.

review.verdict: The Borough Market coffee counter that trained a generation.
review.pull_quote: A flat white and a slab of toast with jam - simple, perfect.
review.body: Right on the edge of Borough Market, Monmouth is standing-room, single-origin, and gloriously no-frills. Grab a filter, a pastry, and lean on a barrel outside. It is a solo-lunch classic and the ideal caffeine stop before wandering the market stalls.

review.verdict: Brutalist icon packing cinema, art, gigs and a jungle-like conservatory.
review.pull_quote: The tropical conservatory alone justifies the trip.
review.body: Europe's largest multi-arts venue is a maze worth getting lost in. Catch a film or exhibition, then find the hidden Conservatory - a humid, plant-stuffed glasshouse tucked above the theatre. The concrete lakeside terrace makes a surprisingly romantic pre-show drink.

review.verdict: Afternoon tea in the pinkest room in London.
review.pull_quote: The egg-shaped loos are worth the booking on their own.
review.body: The Gallery's blush-pink David Shrigley makeover is peak Instagram, but the afternoon tea holds up: tiered stands, immaculate patisserie, theatrical service. Book well ahead. Even a pot of tea and a pastry feels like an event here - proper treat territory.

places.title: Kew Gardens
places.address: Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew, Richmond TW9 3AE

review.verdict: 300 acres of glasshouses, treetop walkways and world-class botany.
review.pull_quote: The Palm House on a cold morning is pure escapism.
review.body: A UNESCO World Heritage garden that rewards a whole day. Do the Victorian Palm House, the Temperate House, and the Treetop Walkway, then picnic on the lawns. Easy on the District line, brilliant with visiting parents, and a proper reset from central London.
